headline: Kenji’s confidence is getting knocked by late direction changes and vague week-to-week expectations, not by lack of effort.
summary_bullets: Late direction changes are the recurring trigger: “The hardest part is changing direction late and feeling like I missed something.”
Clarity is the main gap: “I am still unsure what good really looks like here week to week” and “Some days I feel useful, other days I feel like I am guessing.”
Help-seeking is constrained by self-image: “I do ask for help sometimes, but I worry about sounding slow.”
understanding_paragraph: Kenji is not asking for more work or more attention—he’s asking for a steadier definition of “good” as priorities shift. He feels best when feedback is specific and close to the work, and he loses confidence when priorities change fast. He also holds back on help because he worries it will make him look slow, which keeps uncertainty inside longer than it needs to be.

axes.meaning: Energy dips with uncertainty; late shifts and missed context make the work feel harder than it should.
Motivation fluctuates with confidence, with effort present but ownership getting limited by shaky priorities.
Week-to-week success criteria are unclear, creating guessing and making feedback and priorities feel unreliable.
He’s ready to improve, but the learning loop is unstable because calibration doesn’t happen often enough.

brutal_truth_employee: Kenji is carrying a confidence hit from process churn: “The hardest part is changing direction late and feeling like I missed something.” He also self-censors help—“I do ask for help sometimes, but I worry about sounding slow”—so uncertainty lingers instead of getting resolved early.
brutal_truth_manager: The conversation surfaced a clear signal on clarity (“I am still unsure what good really looks like here week to week”), but the follow-up didn’t lock it into concrete guardrails. Next time, push past “calibration loop” and get him to name the exact cadence, artifacts, and decision points that would make “good” unambiguous.

next_actions.action: Draft a 2-week calibration loop proposal for Kenji: cadence, who attends, and the specific “good looks like” outputs you’ll align on.
Schedule one 30-minute “specific feedback” session with Kenji using one active task; agree on what feedback granularity and timing he needs.
Re-ask the skipped question with a concrete prompt: “What will you change before next week—one behavior, one artifact, or one decision point?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Kenji still says “I am guessing” after the calibration loop starts, the cadence/artifacts are wrong.
Within two weeks: if Kenji stops asking for help entirely (or only asks late), treat “sounding slow” as an active blocker.
